title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface
purpose: Define the intended public API surface for Incursa.Quic using the public surface of System.Net.Quic as the baseline for the initial consumer cut, the first honest server-entry slice, the first honest client-entry slice, the first honest stream-entry slice, the narrow supported write/completion slice, and the first honest initial stream-capacity callback slice.
scope: This specification defines the initial consumer-facing public API surface for Incursa.Quic. The repository is implementing the surface in phases, and the current slice promotes the connection-side facade, the first server-side entry surface (`QuicListener`, `QuicListenerOptions`, `QuicServerConnectionOptions`, `ListenAsync`, and `AcceptConnectionAsync`), the first client-side entry surface (`QuicClientConnectionOptions` and `QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(...)`), the first stream-entry surface (`QuicConnection.AcceptInboundStreamAsync(...)` and `QuicConnection.OpenOutboundStreamAsync(...)`), the narrow runtime-backed stream write/completion slice on send-capable streams, and the first honest initial stream-capacity callback subset over the existing runtime and host machinery. The supported positive client/server loopback boundary is now present on that narrow listener/client path, the supported positive stream open/accept boundary is now present on that same active loopback path through a minimal 1-RTT short-header stream-control lane, the supported stream write/completion boundary is now present on that same path, and the supported stream-capacity callback subset now reports non-zero initial peer stream-capacity increments committed from peer transport parameters. Full TLS trust-store and hostname validation, `IsSupported`, later `MAX_STREAMS`-driven capacity updates, and the broader abort-heavy stream-management follow-ons remain deferred. Helper-layer packet, frame, TLS, handshake, runtime, recovery, and congestion types remain internal unless a future specification explicitly promotes them.
context: The reference surface in Microsoft System.Net.Quic is the strongest baseline for the eventual API, but this repository is deliberately shipping that surface in smaller phases. The target repository already contains a broad internal helper and runtime surface under src/Incursa.Quic, and this specification makes the public/internal boundary explicit so the connection-first facade, the narrow stream-entry follow-on, the narrow write/completion slice, and the initial stream-capacity callback can land without exposing the implementation seams. The client-entry, stream-entry, write/completion, and stream-capacity slices are intentionally honest: they promote the public option bag, connect entry point, stream entry points, the narrow runtime-backed write/completion subset, and one truthful callback subset now, but they do not claim broader data-path success or later stream-capacity evolution beyond what the existing runtime, TLS seam, and minimal 1-RTT application-data lane can really prove.

requirements.title: Limit the public consumer surface to the approved facade types
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose only the consumer-facing facade types named in this specification, and it MUST keep all other current helper, parser, runtime, TLS, handshake, packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and seam types internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: The approved public facade for the current slice is QuicConnection, QuicStream, QuicConnectionOptions, QuicReceiveWindowSizes, QuicAbortDirection, QuicError, QuicException, QuicListener, QuicListenerOptions, QuicClientConnectionOptions, QuicServerConnectionOptions, QuicStreamCapacityChangedArgs, and the corrected QuicStreamType.

The current slice reuses the existing runtime, stream-state, listener-host, runtime-endpoint, and endpoint-host machinery directly. The current surface reuses EndPoint, IPEndPoint, SslApplicationProtocol, SslClientAuthenticationOptions, SslServerAuthenticationOptions, and SslClientHelloInfo directly where needed.

requirements.title: Expose the listener and client entry surfaces honestly
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose server listener creation and accept through QuicListener.ListenAsync(QuicListenerOptions, CancellationToken) and QuicListener.AcceptConnectionAsync(CancellationToken), and it MUST expose client connection establishment through QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(QuicClientConnectionOptions, CancellationToken). The client entry point MUST complete only when the existing runtime reaches the supported connection-establishment boundary, and it MUST otherwise remain pending or fail with an explicit terminal outcome instead of fabricating success.
notes: The listener shell is honest and is backed by the internal listener host.
The client shell is honest and is backed by the existing runtime-endpoint and endpoint-host seams over a connected UDP socket.

This slice now includes the supported positive client/server loopback boundary on the existing listener/client path, including Initial/DCID bootstrap, inbound Initial handling, and listener-side datagram admission.

requirements.title: Keep the connection model opaque and consumer-facing
requirements.statement: The QuicConnection type MUST represent a connected QUIC session and expose the local endpoint, remote endpoint, target host name, negotiated application protocol, negotiated cipher suite, negotiated TLS protocol, remote certificate, AcceptInboundStreamAsync, OpenOutboundStreamAsync, CloseAsync, DisposeAsync, and ToString, while keeping handshake details, packet state, runtime state, and transport-helper state out of the public surface.
notes: The connection object is the consumer's lifecycle anchor for this slice and may be created only by internal wiring, listener accept flow, or the approved client connect flow.
Transport internals should be accessed through the internal runtime, not through public connection properties.
Connection close, dispose, terminal-state projection, and the narrow stream-entry methods are the only lifecycle behaviors this slice promises.
AcceptInboundStreamAsync(...) and OpenOutboundStreamAsync(...) are only honest on the supported active-loopback path that already has the minimal 1-RTT short-header lane in place.
The connection object is not a middleware or event-pipeline surface.

requirements.title: Keep the stream model aligned with standard .NET stream consumers
requirements.statement: The QuicStream type MUST derive from Stream and expose the stream identifier, stream direction, the read-side closed-completion task, a truthful writable-side capability on send-capable streams, and the standard synchronous, asynchronous, span-based, capability, and timeout overrides used by the currently supported consumer slice.
notes: The public QuicStreamType enum MUST use the direction-only model with Bidirectional and Unidirectional values.
The richer internal stream-identity model may continue to track initiator detail separately, but it stays internal.

This slice now makes the stream-entry surface, the read-side/terminal-state surface, and the send-capable writable-side flag backed by QuicConnectionStreamState truthful on the supported loopback path.
The narrow runtime-backed write/completion behavior itself is specified in `REQ-QUIC-API-0010`.
The Stream surface stays internal to the facade for now, and a future slice can decide which timeout and write members are honest once the broader abort-heavy follow-on exists.

requirements.title: Expose configuration through the approved option and callback types
requirements.statement: The library MUST carry configuration through QuicConnectionOptions, QuicListenerOptions, QuicClientConnectionOptions, QuicServerConnectionOptions, and QuicReceiveWindowSizes, and it MUST expose only the approved knobs on those types: close and stream default error codes, handshake and idle timeouts, keep-alive interval, inbound stream limits, the supported initial stream-capacity callback, listener endpoint and backlog, client local/remote endpoints, application protocols, the narrow server connection-options callback, server authentication options, and the supported subset of SslClientAuthenticationOptions.
notes: No custom endpoint or handshake wrapper types are introduced for this slice; the listener and client surfaces reuse EndPoint, IPEndPoint, SslApplicationProtocol, SslClientAuthenticationOptions, SslClientHelloInfo, and SslServerAuthenticationOptions directly.
Public option objects are configuration bags, not live mutable protocol state.
Listener setup, client connect setup, and the server callback are now live.

The supported initial stream-capacity callback subset is intentionally narrow: it reports only non-zero initial peer stream-capacity increments committed from peer transport parameters on the supported loopback path, and the detailed behavior is specified in `REQ-QUIC-API-0009`.

The supported client TLS/auth subset is intentionally narrow: non-empty ALPN, TLS 1.3 or the default protocol selection, no target host / hostname validation, no client certificates or chain policy, an explicit RemoteCertificateValidationCallback gate, and no custom trust, cipher-suite, or renegotiation/resumption overrides. Unsupported SslClientAuthenticationOptions settings MUST be rejected deterministically instead of being silently ignored.
Unsupported SslClientAuthenticationOptions settings MUST be rejected deterministically instead of being silently ignored.
Later `MAX_STREAMS`-driven capacity updates and capacity release after stream close remain deferred to follow-on slices.

requirements.title: Keep the close and error surface explicit
requirements.statement: The library MUST classify terminal and shutdown outcomes through QuicError and QuicException, surface application and transport error codes on the exception, and support per-stream abort direction through QuicAbortDirection so consumers can distinguish read, write, and both-side termination without referencing internal protocol state.
notes: Connection shutdown remains available through QuicConnection.CloseAsync(long errorCode, CancellationToken) and DisposeAsync.
The exception surface is the consumer-facing representation of terminal outcomes, not a protocol debug channel.
CloseAsync validates the application error code and throws ArgumentOutOfRangeException for invalid values.
CloseAsync is terminal and repeated close calls are ignored once the connection is already closed.
DisposeAsync is the consumer-facing cleanup path when no explicit close is issued; it uses the configured default close error code.
Stream abort behavior is deferred until the broader abort-heavy follow-on exists.

requirements.title: Keep the TLS, handshake, packet, and runtime seams internal
requirements.statement: The library MUST keep QuicTlsTranscriptProgress, QuicTlsKeySchedule, QuicTransportTlsBridgeState, QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator, QuicInitialPacketProtection, QuicHandshakePacketProtection, QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial, QuicConnectionRuntime, QuicConnectionRuntimeHost, QuicListenerHost, QuicConnectionRuntimeShard, QuicConnectionSendRuntime, QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint, QuicConnectionEndpointHost, QuicClientConnectionHost, and QuicClientConnectionOptionsValidator internal, and it MUST keep the remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: This boundary keeps listener-host, client-host, runtime-endpoint, endpoint-host, validator, transcript progress, key schedule, bridge state, handshake coordination, packet-protection details, runtime reducer details, stream-state helpers, packet helpers, and transport-parameter helpers out of the consumer contract.
The public API must not expose the existing helper-layer runtime model by accident.

requirements.title: Honor async lifecycle cancellation and terminal-state transitions
requirements.statement: The library MUST honor cancellation tokens on pending listen, accept, connect, open-stream, supported write, and close operations, and the server connection-options callback MUST observe the cancellation token supplied by the listener accept flow. A pre-canceled or later-canceled token MUST complete the still-pending operation with OperationCanceledException, but once a listener accept, connection, or stream has committed or reached a terminal state the public API MUST complete with the documented QuicError or ObjectDisposedException outcome instead of silently reviving or hiding the operation.
notes: The Microsoft tests directly prove cancellation for listener accept, connect, the listener callback, blocked outbound stream opens, and the stream-capacity callback shape; this slice applies that evidence to the listener path, the still-pending client connect path, the stream-entry open/accept path on active loopback connections, the supported write cancellation path, and the supported initial stream-capacity callback subset.
Cancellation does not retroactively undo a committed connection, accepted stream, or completed close.
Pending connect cancellation and disposal must not fabricate handshake completion.
Stream-capacity callback dispatch must not revive a disposed public connection facade.
Idempotent close and dispose semantics are consumer-facing terminal behavior, not a hidden runtime optimization.

requirements.title: Expose the supported initial stream-capacity callback delta
requirements.statement: The library MUST surface the initial peer stream-capacity delta through QuicConnectionOptions.StreamCapacityCallback on the supported loopback establishment path, and it MUST remain silent when the supported boundary is never reached.
notes: The callback is a narrow consumer-visible projection of the existing runtime stream-limit commit bookkeeping and is not a general stream-capacity event pipeline.
The supported subset reports only the initial non-zero peer-advertised outbound stream-open increment committed from peer transport parameters on the supported loopback path.
Later `MAX_STREAMS`-driven updates and stream-close-driven capacity release remain deferred.

requirements.title: Support the narrow runtime-backed stream write and completion lane
requirements.statement: On the supported active loopback path, send-capable QuicStream facades MUST support immediate 1-RTT writes, best-effort graceful write completion through stream disposal, peer read-side byte delivery, and matching EOF observation without implying broader stream parity.
notes: The supported subset is limited to send-capable streams on the active loopback path and is backed by the existing runtime and stream-state seams.
Disposal is best-effort for graceful write completion and does not promise reset/stop-sending parity.
The standard `Stream` flush members remain narrow no-ops on this slice and do not imply extra transport work.

supplemental_sections.heading: Public Surface Inventory
supplemental_sections.content:
- `QuicListener`: server-side listen/accept entry point.
- `QuicConnection`: connected session facade, client connect entry point, and narrow stream-entry point.
- `QuicStream`: standard `Stream` facade for a QUIC stream.
- `QuicConnectionOptions`: shared connection knobs, including the narrow `StreamCapacityCallback` hook.
- `QuicListenerOptions`: listener configuration and narrow server connection-options callback.
- `QuicClientConnectionOptions`: client-side connect configuration.
- `QuicServerConnectionOptions`: server-side accept options.
- `QuicReceiveWindowSizes`: connection and stream receive-window settings.
- `QuicAbortDirection`: stream abort direction.
- `QuicError` and `QuicException`: close and terminal error surface.
- `QuicStreamType`: bidirectional/unidirectional stream direction.
- `QuicStreamCapacityChangedArgs`: initial peer stream-capacity increment payload.
- `QuicConnection.AcceptInboundStreamAsync(...)`: narrow inbound stream accept entry point on the active loopback path.
- `QuicConnection.OpenOutboundStreamAsync(...)`: narrow outbound stream open entry point on the active loopback path.
- `QuicConnectionOptions.StreamCapacityCallback`: narrow initial peer stream-capacity callback.

The public API should reuse `EndPoint`, `IPEndPoint`, `SslApplicationProtocol`, `SslClientAuthenticationOptions`, `SslClientHelloInfo`, and `SslServerAuthenticationOptions` directly rather than introduce new endpoint or TLS wrapper abstractions.

supplemental_sections.heading: Behavioral Evidence From Microsoft Tests
supplemental_sections.content:
The following Microsoft library-focused tests were the behavioral baseline for this slice:

- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicListenerTests.cs`: listener setup validation, callback exception and timeout behavior, cancellation, backlog, ALPN, and listener-disposal behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicConnectionTests.cs`: connect validation, close/dispose terminal-state behavior, stream-capacity callback deltas, peer-certificate lifetime, peer-abort propagation, and stream-entry open/accept behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamTests.cs`: direction-based capabilities, EOF and graceful completion, abort mapping, completion-task behavior, cancellation, stream-dispose behavior, and the shape of accepted/created stream facades.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`: `QuicStream` participates in the standard timeout-aware `Stream` contract.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`: the API reuses BCL TLS options directly and surfaces invalid cipher-suite combinations through public exceptions.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicTests.cs`: idle timeout, blocked outbound stream open cancellation, and idempotent close behavior.

These tests justified the listener validation, accept cancellation/disposal, and callback-token language in `REQ-QUIC-API-0002`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0005`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0008`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0009`, while also continuing to justify the lifecycle, stream-entry, write/completion, and cancellation language in `REQ-QUIC-API-0003`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0004`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0006`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0010`.

supplemental_sections.heading: Internal-Only Surface
supplemental_sections.content:
The current helper and runtime seam types stay internal, including `QuicTlsTranscriptProgress`, `QuicTlsKeySchedule`, `QuicTransportTlsBridgeState`, `QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator`, `QuicInitialPacketProtection`, `QuicHandshakePacketProtection`, `QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeHost`, `QuicListenerHost`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeShard`, `QuicConnectionSendRuntime`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint`, `QuicConnectionEndpointHost`, `QuicClientConnectionHost`, and `QuicClientConnectionOptionsValidator`.

The remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers also remain internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes one of them.

supplemental_sections.heading: Deviation Policy
supplemental_sections.content:
No intentional deviations from the Microsoft ref surface are required for this slice.

The repo-specific rule is that the richer internal transport engine stays hidden behind the consumer facade, the supported client TLS/auth subset is explicit and reject-first, and the public surface does not grow into a general middleware model.
